bibliographic_data.title: The Energy Complexity of Broadcast
bibliographic_data.abstract: Energy is often the most constrained resource in networks of battery-powered devices, and as devices become smaller, they spend a larger fraction of their energy on communication (transceiver usage) not computation. As an imperfect proxy for true energy usage, we define energy complexity to be the number of time slots a device transmits/listens; idle time and computation are free. In this paper we investigate the energy complexity of fundamental communication primitives such as Broadcast in multi-hop radio networks. We consider models with collision detection (CD) and without (No-CD), as well as both randomized and deterministic algorithms.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper investigates the energy complexity of Broadcast in multi-hop radio networks, showing connections to time complexity and providing nearly optimal solutions for both measures simultaneously.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors define energy complexity as the number of time slots a device transmits/listens, with idle time and computation being free. They consider models with collision detection (CD) and without (No-CD), as well as randomized and deterministic algorithms to establish lower and upper bounds on Broadcast.
outcomes: The energy complexity of Broadcast in multi-hop networks is connected to the time complexity of LeaderElection in single-hop networks, leading to lower bounds of Ω(log n) and Ω(log2 n) for CD and No-CD models respectively.

Nearly optimal solutions are provided with O( log n log log n / log log log n ) energy and O(log3 n) time for the CD model, and O( logO(1/ϵ) n ) energy and O(D1+ϵ logO(1/ϵ) n) time for any constant ϵ > 0 in both models.
For path graphs with unknown positions, a provably optimal Broadcast algorithm is provided taking O(n) time and expected O(log n) energy.
core_content.full_text_markdown: In many networks of small wireless devices, the most constrained resource is energy, often spent on radio transceiver usage—sending and receiving packets—not computation. This paper investigates the energy complexity of fundamental communication primitives such as Broadcast in multi-hop radio networks. The authors define energy complexity as the number of time slots a device transmits/listens, with idle time and computation being free. They consider models with collision detection (CD) and without (No-CD), as well as randomized and deterministic algorithms to establish lower and upper bounds on Broadcast. Key findings include connections between time complexity in single-hop networks and energy complexity in multi-hop networks, nearly optimal solutions for both measures simultaneously, and a provably optimal algorithm for path graphs with unknown positions.
